text: The robotic-assisted surgical procedure involves the excision of a type I choledochal cyst, followed by the creation of a hepatico-duodenostomy to connect the hepatic duct to the duodenum.
A fifteen-year-old female with BMI of forty-five and no other significant medical history underwent workup for recurrent right upper quadrant abdominal pain.
Ultrasonography detected extrahepatic cystic lesion.
Subsequent magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography demonstrated a three point seven by three point zero centimeter type one choledochal cyst.
Thus, she underwent robotic assisted excision of choledochal cysts with hepatico-duodenostomy.

The surgeon places a 12mm umbilical trocar, followed by an 8mm robotic trocar below the left costal margin in the midclavicular line. Another 8mm robotic trocar is positioned at the umbilicus in the right midclavicular line, and a third 8mm robotic trocar is placed in the right flank above the second trocar. A 5mm step trocar is inserted between the first robotic trocar and the umbilical trocar as an assistant port.
The surgeon uses hook electrocautery to divide extensive adhesions between the omentum and the gallbladder, separating the two structures.
The surgeon follows the gallbladder infundibulum and cystic duct to the choledochal cyst. The cystic duct is clipped and divided using robotic endoshears.

The surgeon uses appropriate retraction and meticulous blunt and electrocautery dissection to circumferentially separate the choledochal cyst from adhesions to the duodenum and surrounding soft tissues.

The surgeon periodically decompresses the choledochal cyst by removing and replacing the cystic duct clip. The cyst is dissected laterally, medially, and posteriorly until it is completely mobilized and freed from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully dissects the cyst as it abuts the portal vein, ensuring precise separation from the adjacent vascular structure.
A circumferential umbilical tape is placed around the surgical site to maintain retraction, aiding in the visualization and manipulation of the gallbladder and surrounding structures.
The surgeon continues distal dissection of the choledochal cyst, revealing a sharp tapering of the cyst to approximately two to three millimeters near the pancreatic head.
The surgeon ligates the common bile duct using 3-0 PDS and 3-0 Vicryl sutures, then divides the duct after placing a proximal clip.
The surgeon applies traction on the cystic duct stump and manipulates the distal portion of the cyst, completing the dissection from the proximal portal vein towards the porta hepatis.
The surgeon mobilizes the first and proximal second portions of the duodenum, carefully freeing these sections from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the cyst half a centimeter distal to the confluence of the right and left hepatic ducts, using precise surgical technique to separate the structure.
The surgeon creates a one-centimeter duodenotomy five centimeters distal to the pylorus in an anti-mesenteric longitudinal fashion, revealing the interior of the duodenum.

The surgeon performs a hand-sewn interrupted hepatico-duodenostomy, using three ovicle sutures. Four stitches are placed on the posterior wall and six stitches on the anterior wall, connecting the hepatic duct to the duodenum.
The surgeon completes the cholecystectomy, with the gallbladder fully removed from its attachments and the surgical field cleared.
A surgical drain is placed at the anastomotic site at the end of the procedure.
Post-operative course, the patient tolerated the procedure well and was transferred to the regular nursing floor for recovery.
NG tube was removed on post-operative day three.
Diet was advanced as tolerated with return of bowel function.
She was discharged on post-operative day five after removal of the abdominal drain that was placed.
She was doing well five weeks following surgery.
Pathology showed type one choledochal cyst with no dysplasia.
